Statements of Economic Interesl 
322.5660 
322·5901 
322-6441 
322-6444 
Honorable Larry Stirling 
State Assembly 
State Capitol 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Dear Assemblyman Stirling: 
April 2, 1985 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-85-045 
Thank you for your letter requesting advice concerning your 
reporting duties under the Political Reform Act.11 
QUESTION 
Are you required to report on your Statement of Economic 
Interests transportation, food, and lodging provided to you and 
your wife by the government of South Africa in connection with 
your trip to South Africa to determine whether the State of 
California should divest its investments in that country? 
CONCLUSION 
The cost of any transportation, food, and lodging provided 
to you by the government of South Africa must be reported as a 
gift on your Statement of Economic Interests if the total amount 
of benefits you received was in excess of fifty dollars ($50). 
DISCUSSION 
Section 82030 states the general rule that "income" for the 
purposes of the Political Reform Act includes any reimbursement 
for expenses. 
Although Section 82030 (b) (2) specifies that 
reimbursement received from a state, local, or federal 
11 
Government Code Sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory 
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise 
indicated. 


Honorable Larry Stirling 
April 2, 1985 
Page 2 
government agency is excepted from the definition of income, 
this exception does not apply to your situation because you 
received reimbursement from a foreign government. 
More 
specifically, you received a gift from the government of South 
Africa when you accepted transportation, food, and lodging paid 
for by that government, and you did not provide consideration of 
equal or greater value. 
Section 82028. 
The Commission has ruled that, under specific 
circumstances, payment of a public official's transportation and 
other expenses by an entity other than a state, local, or 
federal government agency may be considered a gift to the 
government agency rather than to the public official. 
In its 
opinion issued to Peter G. Stone, 3 FPPC Opinions 52 (No. 
77-003, June 9, 1977), the Commission set forth the following 
four minimum criteria for determining when a gift is made to the 
government agency rather than to the official: 
1. 
The aonor intended to donate the gift to the public 
agency and not to the official; 
2. 
The public agency exercises substantial control over 
use of the gift; 
3. 
The aonor has not limited use of the gift to specified 
or high-level employees, but rather has made it generally 
available to the public agency's personnel in connection with 
agency business without regard to official status; and 
4. 
The making and use of the gift was formalized in a 
resolution of the public agency (a written public record will 
suffice for administrative agencies not possessing the 
legislative power of adopting resolutions) which embodies the 
standards set forth above. 
In the Stone Opinion, the Commission stated that these four 
criteria are indications that the gift was made to the public 
agency only, without providing any significant or unusual 
benefit to the public official. 
The Commission indicated that 
when the four criteria are met, the public official receives a 
benefit that is analogous to reimbursement for expenses or per 
diem from a state, local, or federal government agency, items 
which are not reportable pursuant to Section 82030(b) (2). 
The criteria of the Stone Opinion were not met in your 
case; therefore, the payment for your transportation and 
expenses constituted a gift to you rather than a gift to the 
state. 


Honorable Larry Stirling 
April 2, 1985 
Page 3 
You are required to disclose each source of gifts in excess 
of fifty dollars ($50), and the amount and date of the gifts, on 
your Statement of Economic Interests,. 
Section 87302(b). 
Section 81011 also requires that when the amount of goods, 
services, facilities, or anything of value other than money is 
required to be reported, the amount shall be the estimated fair 
market value at the time received or expended, and a description 
of the goods, services, or facilities shall be appended to the 
report or statement. 
Therefore, you must describe the gifts you 
received, and disclose the estimated fair market value and date 
of the gifts. 
In general, you are not required to report gifts made to 
your spouse. 
Cory Opinion, 2 FPPC Opinions 48 {No. 75-094-A, 
April 22, 1976}. 
Therefore, you are not required to disclose 
the cost of transportation, food, and lodging provided to your 
wife by the government of South Africa. 
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, 
please contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
KED:plh 
Very truly yours, 
tj;i1v;'~(k- r . ~~~ 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
Counsel 
Legal Division

SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 
PHONE: (916) 445-6161 
State of California

LARRY STIRLING

Sacramento, CA 
95804 
Gentlemen:

AID PLAZA BUILDING 
7777 ALVARADO ROAD - SUITE 315 
LA MESA, CA 92041 
PHONE: (619) 237-7777 
From September 21 through October 13, 1984, my wife and I 
visited South Africa as official guests of the Department of 
Foreign Affairs. 
The purpose of the trip was to determine whether the State of 
California should divest its investments in that country. 
Enclosed is a letter regarding the expenses for the trip. 
Could 
you please provide a written detailed opinion as to whether these 
expenses are reportable as a gift on my annual Statement of 
Economic Interests. 
Please send a copy of the opinion to the 
office of the Legislative Counsel. 
As the report is due March 1, I would appreciate your 
immediate reply. 
Thank you. 
Z~d~ 
Assemblyman, 77th District 
LS:dkk 
Representing the Cities of EI Cajon, La Mesa, San Diego and Santee

JAN 11 1985

wife are estimated as follows,

your trip to South Africa to determine whether the State of 
California should divest its investments in that country?

The cost of any transportation, food, and lodging provided 
to you by the government of South Africa must be reported as a 
gift on your Statement of Economic Interests if the total amount 
of benefits you received was in excess of fifty dollars ($50).
